description: Installation QC checklists, tubing walkdown, calibration QA/QC, loop check documentation, ITCC/turnover packages, punch list categories, inspection hold points, and SIS validation for instrumentation field work

mounting: Bracket secure to pipe stand or structural steel — no wobble, all bolts torqued
Mounting height accessible for calibration (3ft to 5ft preferred — tech shouldn't need scaffold for routine cal)
Bracket material matches spec (galvanized, stainless, painted CS per project requirement)
Vibration isolation pad installed if on reciprocating equipment (compressors, pumps)
orientation: Terminal housing rotated so conduit entry faces DOWN or sideways — never UP (rain/condensation drains into electronics)
Display readable from grade or platform without contortion
For DP transmitters: high-side and low-side connected to correct impulse legs — VERIFY with P&ID, not assumption
Electronics housing can be rotated independent of process connection on most models — use this

impulse_line_slope.liquid_service: Slope DOWN from process tap to transmitter, minimum 1 inch per foot. Transmitter BELOW tap. NO high points — air pockets cause erratic readings.
impulse_line_slope.gas_service: Slope UP from process tap to transmitter, minimum 1 inch per foot. Transmitter ABOVE tap. NO low points — liquid traps cause offset and sluggish response.
impulse_line_slope.steam_service: Condensate pots at process connection, filled with water. Both legs SAME length, same routing (same heat exposure). Unequal legs = permanent offset.
impulse_line_slope.common_mistake: Running impulse lines level 'because it looks neat' — this GUARANTEES trapped air or liquid. The slope is not optional.

manifold_valve_position: 3-valve or 5-valve manifold installed between process and transmitter
Block valves OPEN, equalizing valve CLOSED for normal operation
Verify valve handle positions match expected — a closed block valve reads 0 DP and the DCS shows a false reading
5-valve manifold: verify vent valves are capped and closed after initial fill
process_connection: Correct flange rating matches process design pressure (150#, 300#, 600#, etc.)
Gasket material per spec — spiral wound for flanged, soft iron for RTJ
Flange bolts torqued to spec — star pattern, not sequential
Thread sealant on NPT connections — Teflon tape (3 wraps clockwise when facing threads) or pipe dope per spec
For direct-mount transmitters: diaphragm seal clean, no damage to diaphragm face, fill fluid level correct
tag_verification: Stainless steel nameplate wired to instrument with SS wire (not zip ties — they degrade in UV)
Tag number matches instrument index, P&ID, and loop diagram — check ALL three, mismatches happen
Tag clearly visible and readable from access side
Stamped or engraved — not handwritten marker (fades in weeks outside)
sunshade: Required on ALL outdoor electronic transmitters unless project spec says otherwise
Positioned to shade the electronics housing from direct sun — not just sitting on top of the bracket
Check that sunshade doesn't block conduit entry or terminal access
Most common punch item in hot climates — gets forgotten because it's 'just a sunshade'

flow_direction: Arrow on valve body matches P&ID flow direction — THIS IS THE MOST COMMON CONTROL VALVE INSTALLATION ERROR
For globe valves: flow-under-plug vs flow-over-plug per data sheet (affects shutoff and stability)
If no arrow on body, check valve data sheet for flow direction requirements
Butterfly valves: verify disc rotation direction gives correct fail position
tag_match: Body tag, actuator tag, and positioner tag all reference same tag number
Verify valve size matches line size or spec (reduced trim valves may look too small — check data sheet)
Confirm valve CV rating matches the spec — wrong valve from warehouse is a real problem on large projects
actuator_air_supply: Air supply pressure matches actuator requirement (typically 60-80 PSI instrument air)
Air supply tubing connected and tight — check for leaks with snoop/soap solution
Filter/regulator installed on air supply, set to correct pressure, drain leg on filter
Volume booster installed if specified (large actuators need fast stroking)
Air supply isolation valve installed and OPEN — label it clearly
fail_position_verified: Remove air supply — valve must go to specified fail position (FC, FO, or FL)
Fail-Closed (FC): valve goes fully shut on air loss — spring return, verify tight shutoff if required
Fail-Open (FO): valve goes fully open on air loss — spring return
Fail-Last (FL): valve stays at last position on air loss — lock-up valve or volume tank installed and tested
DOCUMENT the test: date, who verified, actual position achieved, time to full stroke
Common gotcha: handwheel left engaged overrides the fail position — always verify handwheel is backed off after manual operation
handwheel_operation: Verify handwheel turns freely and actually moves the valve stem
Confirm CW = close, CCW = open (or as labeled) — document this if non-standard
Verify handwheel is NOT engaged (declutched) during normal automatic operation
Check that handwheel override doesn't interfere with actuator stroke
packing_bolts: Packing bolts evenly torqued — no visible stem leakage (process or air)
Live-loaded packing: Belleville springs compressed to proper stack height
Packing follower is level — uneven tightening causes stem binding
Environmental packing (double packing with lantern ring and leak-off) installed if required for fugitive emissions
shipping_stops_removed: ALL shipping stops, travel blocks, and shipping bolts REMOVED before commissioning
This is a checklist item because if forgotten the valve will not stroke and the crew wastes hours troubleshooting 'bad positioner' or 'bad actuator' before finding a $2 bolt
Check actuator yoke, diaphragm casing, and any bolted travel stops
Some rotary valves have packing protectors or disc lock plates — remove these too

thermowell_first: Thermowell MUST be installed and hydrotest-proven BEFORE the sensor goes in
Verify thermowell material matches spec (316SS, Inconel, Hastelloy per process conditions)
Thermowell insertion length correct per data sheet — too short reads ambient, too long creates vibration/breakage risk
Thermowell bore size matches sensor diameter (1/4-inch or 3/8-inch typical)
Lagging extension on thermowell if line is insulated — sensor head must be OUTSIDE insulation
correct_depth: Sensor bottomed out in thermowell with spring-loaded connection (ensures thermal contact)
For thermowells in large vessels: verify U-dimension (insertion length) reaches into process flow, not dead zone near wall
Pipe thermowells: tip should reach 1/3 to 1/2 pipe diameter into flow for representative reading
Check for thermowell too long for the pipe — tip hits far wall in small pipes, creates mechanical stress
extension_wire_match: Thermocouple extension wire type MUST match TC type (K to K, J to J, T to T — NO mixing)
Color code verification: Type K = Yellow jacket, Type J = Black jacket, Type T = Blue jacket (ANSI)
RED is ALWAYS negative in US ANSI standard — this is backwards from most other wiring conventions and causes polarity errors
NEVER use copper wire to extend a thermocouple — you get the reading at the junction where copper meets TC wire, not at the sensor
For RTDs: 3-wire or 4-wire configuration per spec, all lead wires same gauge and length for resistance matching
head_orientation: Connection head tilted so conduit entry is at side or bottom — NOT pointing up (water ingress)
Terminal block accessible when head cover is removed
If head is in insulated area, verify cable exits insulation without sharp bends
connection_polarity: Thermocouple: positive to positive, negative to negative at every junction (head, JB, marshalling) — one reversal makes the reading go the wrong direction
Verify with a millivolt check: heat sensor slightly, confirm mV goes positive at correct terminal
RTD: identify and label all leads (3-wire: two same-color = compensating leads, one different = signal)
Crimped or welded TC junctions only — no twist-and-tape in the field, it creates a secondary junction

bevel_downstream: Bevel (sharp edge) faces UPSTREAM into flow. Bored-out side (counterbore) faces DOWNSTREAM.
Paddle/tab text is readable from upstream side — text faces the flow
THIS IS THE SINGLE MOST COMMON ORIFICE PLATE ERROR — verify by looking at the bevel with a flashlight through the flange gap before tightening
If installed backwards: DP reading will be wrong (typically reads higher than actual), flow calculation is invalid
correct_bore: Measure bore diameter with calipers — verify against data sheet specification
Check plate tag stamping matches the data sheet: bore, pipe size, material, tag number
Beta ratio (bore/pipe ID) should match calculations — wrong bore means wrong flow reading
Inspect bore edge for nicks, burrs, or corrosion — damaged edge creates inaccurate readings
gasket_not_protruding: Gasket ID must be LARGER than orifice bore — any protrusion into the flow stream is a rejection
Use specified gasket material and thickness per spec (RTJ, spiral wound, or soft cut)
After torquing flanges, visually confirm gasket is not visible in the bore opening
Spiral wound gaskets with inner ring prevent this — specify inner ring for critical measurements
straight_run_requirements: Minimum upstream: 10D for single elbow, 20D for two elbows in different planes, 30D for reducer — per AGA/ASME standards or project spec
Minimum downstream: 5D typical, 8D if control valve is downstream
Flow conditioner installed if straight run cannot be met — verify conditioner is correct size and properly oriented
Measure from orifice flange center to nearest upstream disturbance — DON'T guess, actually measure
Thermowell or test connection within straight run counts as a disturbance — check spec for allowances
drain_vent_connections: Liquid service: vent connections on top of impulse lines (to bleed trapped gas)
Gas service: drain connections on bottom of impulse lines (to drain trapped liquid)
Steam service: condensate pots at taps, properly filled
Verify taps are on correct side of orifice (upstream = high, downstream = low) per P&ID
Root valves at process taps installed and operational

mounting_bracket_plumb: Bracket plumb and level within 1 degree — a tilted DP transmitter or displacer reads wrong
For external float chambers: chamber must be plumb, mounting flanges aligned with nozzles
All mounting bolts installed — not 3 out of 4 'because it's tight enough'
Anti-vibration measures on reciprocating equipment or near high-vibration areas
nozzle_alignment: Upper and lower nozzle elevations match P&ID and vessel drawing
Nozzle projection into vessel correct — not protruding past vessel wall liner if present
For external chambers: nozzle connection valves open, drain valve closed
Verify level range matches the distance between nozzle centers (or data sheet URV/LRV)
radar_antenna_clearance: Antenna cone angle clear of vessel walls, agitators, baffles, heating coils, ladders
Minimum distance from wall: typically 1/6 of tank diameter or 12 inches, whichever is greater
Non-metallic standoff pipe for guided wave radar extends below minimum process level
Still pipe installed if specified — verify inside diameter is correct for radar frequency
No nozzle extension that creates false echo — antenna tip should be at or below nozzle flange face
calibration_range_matches_vessel: Transmitter 0% = vessel empty level (at lower nozzle for DP, bottom of measurement range for radar)
Transmitter 100% = vessel full level (at upper nozzle for DP, top of measurement range for radar)
For DP level: factor in specific gravity of process fluid — SG change means level reading change
Verify units: inches of water column, PSI, mmH2O — must match DCS configuration
Suppression/elevation applied correctly if transmitter is above or below lower tap

slope_requirements.liquid_service: Slope DOWN from process to transmitter — minimum 1 inch per foot. Transmitter below tap. Every high point is a potential air trap.
slope_requirements.gas_service: Slope UP from process to transmitter — minimum 1 inch per foot. Transmitter above tap. Every low point collects condensate.
slope_requirements.steam_condensate_legs: Both legs same length, same routing, same heat exposure. Fill before putting in service.
slope_requirements.common_error: Running tubing level to make it look neat on the rack. Looks professional, reads wrong.

support_spacing.general_rule: Support within 6 inches of every change of direction, fitting, and valve
support_spacing.support_material: Match tubing material or use approved cushioned clamps (no bare carbon steel clamps on stainless tubing — galvanic corrosion)
support_spacing.common_problem: Tubing sagging between supports on hot days (thermal expansion) — install with slight droop allowance

no_kinks: Any visible kink, flatten, or buckle is a REJECT — tubing must be replaced, not straightened
A kink restricts flow and creates a weak point that fails under pressure or vibration
Kinked tubing also creates a trap for liquids or gases depending on orientation
Inspect entire run, especially around bends and where tubing exits conduit or passes through penetrations

bending_radius.minimum: 5 times the outside diameter (5x OD) for stainless steel tubing
bending_radius.half_inch_minimum: 2.5 inches minimum bend radius for 1/2-inch OD tubing
bending_radius.quarter_inch_minimum: 1.25 inches minimum bend radius for 1/4-inch OD tubing
bending_radius.method: Use a proper tube bender — NEVER bend by hand or over a pipe. Hand bends kink or create flat spots.
bending_radius.spring_back: Overbend slightly — tubing springs back 2-5 degrees after bending
bending_radius.inspection: After bending, roll tubing on flat surface — any flat spot or ovality is rejectable

labeling: Both ends of every tube labeled with instrument tag number and service description
Labels are stainless steel tags with SS wire, or permanent UV-resistant markers on approved label tape
Label at each end plus at every penetration (wall, floor, tray) — if you can't trace it, it's not done
Color coding per project spec: blue for instrument air, yellow for sample, etc.
capped_when_not_connected: All open tube ends capped with plastic or metal caps when not actively being worked on
This prevents debris, insects, and moisture from entering the tubing
Caps removed only at time of final connection — not the day before, not the week before
Cap removal and final connection should be documented on the tubing walkdown checklist

pressure_testing.test_medium: Typically nitrogen (N2) or instrument air — NEVER use oxygen or compressed air from shop compressor (oil contamination)
pressure_testing.test_pressure: 1.5x design pressure, or per project spec (typically 150 PSI for instrument air systems)
pressure_testing.hold_time: Minimum 10 minutes at test pressure with no drop — or per spec (some require 1 hour or 4 hours)
pressure_testing.leak_check: Apply snoop or soap solution to every fitting, valve, and connection during test
pressure_testing.documentation: Record test pressure, hold time, medium, pass/fail, date, tech signature, witness signature
pressure_testing.common_failure: Ferrule not properly seated — remake fitting with new ferrules, not just retighten

blow_through.purpose: Verify tubing is clean and unobstructed before connecting to instrument
blow_through.method: Apply air or N2 at one end, verify flow exits the other end — feel it with your hand or use a tissue
blow_through.when: After pressure test, before final instrument connection
blow_through.why_it_matters: Debris from installation (metal shavings from cutting, dirt, tape residue) plugs transmitter diaphragms and valve orifices
blow_through.common_find: Teflon tape threads that entered the tube during fitting makeup — this is why you don't over-apply tape

as_found_readings.definition: The readings recorded BEFORE any adjustment is made — this is the instrument's actual condition in the field
as_found_readings.why_critical: As-found data proves whether the instrument was reading correctly during operation, or if process data from the previous period was unreliable
as_found_readings.also_record: Any alarm or trip points, hysteresis (up vs down readings), response time if specified
as_found_readings.do_not_adjust_first: NEVER touch zero or span before recording as-found. If the tech adjusts first, as-found data is lost and the calibration certificate is invalid.

as_left_readings.definition: The readings recorded AFTER adjustment/calibration is complete
as_left_readings.must_be_within_tolerance: As-left readings must be within specified tolerance band or the calibration fails
as_left_readings.record_same_points: Same test points as as-found for direct comparison
as_left_readings.up_and_down: Record both increasing (upscale) and decreasing (downscale) readings to check for hysteresis

tolerance_bands.transmitters_general: +/- 0.1% of calibrated span (high-accuracy transmitters may be +/- 0.04%)
tolerance_bands.pressure_gauges: +/- 1% of full scale (ASME B40.100 Grade 2A) or +/- 0.5% (Grade 3A)
tolerance_bands.temperature_transmitters: +/- 0.1% of span or +/- 0.5 degrees (whichever is greater per spec)
tolerance_bands.safety_instrumented_systems: Tighter than BPCS — typically +/- 0.5% of span for SIS transmitters, per SIL verification report
tolerance_bands.flow_meters: Depends on type: orifice +/- 1-2% of rate, mag meter +/- 0.5%, Coriolis +/- 0.1%
tolerance_bands.important_note: Always check PROJECT spec for tolerance — the numbers above are industry typical but your project may be tighter or looser

calibration_certificate_requirements: Instrument tag number and serial number
Date of calibration and due date for next calibration
As-found readings at each test point
As-left readings at each test point
Test equipment used: manufacturer, model, serial number, cal due date
QC inspector signature (if required by ITP)
Environmental conditions during calibration (temperature if accuracy is temperature-dependent)

nist_traceability.requirement: All test equipment must be traceable to NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) through an unbroken chain of calibrations
nist_traceability.chain: NIST primary standard -> accredited lab reference standard -> working standard -> field calibrator
nist_traceability.calibrator_sticker: Every field calibrator must have a current calibration sticker with due date visible — expired calibrator invalidates ALL calibrations performed with it
nist_traceability.audit_trail: Maintain calibration certificates for all test equipment — auditors WILL ask for these
nist_traceability.common_problem: Borrowing a calibrator from another crew without checking its cal date — if it's expired, every cal you did that day gets redone

required: DCS/PLC operator or technician (verifies control system side)
Both must sign the loop check sheet — one signature is not a complete loop check
optional_but_common: QC inspector (if loop check is a witness or hold point per ITP)
Client representative (on some projects, owner witnesses safety loops)

signal_path: Signal flows correctly from field device through junction boxes, marshalling cabinets, to DCS/PLC I/O card
Correct channel, correct tag, correct engineering units displayed
Signal direction: transmitter to DCS (input) or DCS to valve (output)
alarms: Each configured alarm tested at its setpoint
Alarm appears on correct DCS operator screen with correct priority (high, high-high, low, low-low)
Alarm text is correct and meaningful to operator
interlocks: Each interlock tested at its trip point
Correct action taken (pump stop, valve close, compressor shutdown, etc.)
Interlock resets correctly after trip condition clears
Interlock bypass/override tested (if applicable — must be controlled and documented)
fail_safe: Remove power or signal — verify instrument and final element go to safe state
For valves: confirm fail position (FC/FO/FL) matches P&ID and control narrative
For transmitters: verify burnout direction (upscale/downscale) matches DCS configuration

pass_fail_criteria.pass: All test points verified correct, all alarms trigger at setpoints, all interlocks function, fail-safe confirmed, documentation complete and signed
pass_fail_criteria.conditional_pass: Minor discrepancies that don't affect safety or operation — document as punch items to be resolved before handover
pass_fail_criteria.fail: Any safety function does not work, signal path incorrect, alarms missing or at wrong setpoint, fail-safe position wrong — MUST be corrected and retested

punch_items_generated: Any deficiency found during loop check becomes a punch item with: tag number, description, category (A/B/C), responsible party, due date
Category A: prevents operation — must be resolved before first oil/gas/product
Category B: does not prevent operation but must be resolved before final turnover
Category C: cosmetic or minor — resolved during warranty period
ALL punch items tracked in a database or register with status updates

itcc_requirements.definition: Instrument Test, Calibration, and Completion — the documentation package that proves each instrument is installed, calibrated, tested, and ready for service

contents_per_instrument.instrument_index_number: Tag number, service description, P&ID number, area/unit number, loop number — must match across ALL documents
contents_per_instrument.pid_reference: P&ID revision number where instrument appears — must be latest issued-for-construction revision
contents_per_instrument.calibration_certificate: Attached and signed — as-found, as-left, within tolerance, test equipment traceable
contents_per_instrument.loop_check_signed: Both field tech and DCS operator signatures, all test results recorded, pass/fail noted
contents_per_instrument.as_built_redlines: Any field changes marked on the loop diagram, interconnect drawing, or installation detail — WHERE the instrument actually is vs WHERE the drawing said it would be
contents_per_instrument.preservation_status: Instrument preserved per manufacturer requirements — desiccant bags, caps on connections, power disconnected, preservation log current

common_itcc_rejections: Missing calibration certificate — tech calibrated but didn't fill out the paperwork
Wrong revision of reference document — P&ID was revised but ITCC still references old revision
Loop check missing second signature — DCS operator wasn't available, tech signed both lines (not acceptable)
As-built redlines not done — 'it was installed per drawing' is rarely true and must be verified
Test equipment calibration expired — invalidates the calibration certificate

per_instrument: Instrument data sheet (with any field-marked corrections)
Loop diagram (as-built redlined if changes were made)
Spare parts list (with part numbers and recommended stock quantities)
Material certificates for wetted parts (MTR/mill test reports for process-contact materials)
per_loop: Loop check sheet (signed by field tech and DCS operator)
Alarm and trip point verification record
SIS proof test record (if safety loop)
per_system: Instrument index (master list of all instruments in the system)
Cable schedule and cable pull records
common_missing_items: Vendor manuals — often shipped with equipment and lost during construction
Material certificates — need to be requested from procurement at time of purchase, not at turnover
Spare parts — ordered late or not at all, delays turnover
As-built drawings — redlining not done during construction, now nobody remembers what changed

tagging: Missing instrument tag (fell off, never installed, wrong material degraded)
Tag doesn't match drawing (typo, old revision, wrong loop number)
Tag not readable (marker faded, stamping too light, covered by insulation)
Tag wired with carbon steel wire on stainless instrument (galvanic corrosion)
sunshades: Sunshade installed but not positioned to actually shade the electronics
Sunshade material incorrect (should be stainless, got galvanized)
tubing_supports: Missing support — tubing span exceeds 5 feet
Support within 6 inches of direction change missing
Carbon steel clamp on stainless tubing (galvanic corrosion)
junction_box_covers: Cover missing or not secured (rain, dust, insects enter and destroy terminations)
Cover gasket missing or damaged (IP rating compromised)
Cover bolts missing — 1 out of 4 bolts is not acceptable
Breather/drain plug missing (pressure builds up inside from temperature cycling)
conduit_seals: Conduit seal (Chico/compound seal) missing at hazardous area boundary
Seal not poured or not fully filled
Conduit drain at low point missing (water collects and wicks into instruments/JBs)
Conduit seal dam not installed (compound runs out during pouring)
cable_labels: Cable not labeled at both ends
Label material degrading (paper labels in outdoor environment)
Label at junction box but not at marshalling cabinet (or vice versa)
calibration_stickers: Sticker information incomplete (missing tech name, cal date, or due date)
Sticker not matching calibration certificate on file
general_installation: Instrument air not connected or not turned on
Drain/vent valve missing or not capped
Process connection valve left closed (technician forgot to open root valve)
Instrument not preserved (desiccant missing, caps off, exposed to weather)
Cable gland not tight (water ingress path)
Unused cable entries not plugged (breaks hazardous area certification)
Earth/ground wire not connected or loose

H_hold.definition: HOLD point — work STOPS and CANNOT proceed until inspector physically inspects and signs release
H_hold.consequence_of_proceeding: Work done past an unreleased Hold point must be torn out and redone — this is non-negotiable
typical_instrument_H_points: Hydrostatic test of impulse tubing systems
SIS loop function test (safety instrumented systems)
Final SIS validation before SIL sign-off
Control valve seat leakage test (if specified as H)
H_hold.process: Submit inspection request 24-48 hours in advance. Inspector reviews documentation BEFORE going to field. Work stops at hold point until release.

W_witness.definition: WITNESS point — inspector is invited to attend. If inspector does not attend after proper notification, work MAY proceed.
W_witness.notification_requirement: Written notification (email or inspection request form) at least 24 hours in advance — keep the notification as proof
W_witness.documentation: Record whether inspector was present or absent. If absent, note notification was given.

R_review.definition: REVIEW point — inspector reviews documentation only, does not need to be present for the work
R_review.turnaround: Submit documents to inspector — typical review period 3-5 business days. Plan ahead.

inspection_hold_points.itp_reference: All hold, witness, and review points are defined in the Inspection and Test Plan (ITP) — THIS document governs what gets inspected and when. Get a copy at project start and track every point.

proof_test_procedures.purpose: Verify that safety instrumented function (SIF) works end-to-end: from sensor detecting dangerous condition through logic solver to final element taking safe action
proof_test_procedures.scope: Tests the ENTIRE safety function — not just individual instruments
full_proof_test: Simulate the process condition at the sensor (apply test pressure, temperature, level)
Verify sensor detects the condition and transmits correct signal
Verify logic solver receives signal and executes programmed action within required time
Verify final element (valve, relay, motor contactor) takes the correct safe-state action
Verify alarms annunciate correctly in control room
Time the entire response from sensor input to final element action — must meet required SIF response time
proof_test_procedures.frequency: Determined by SIL level and PFD calculations — typically annually for SIL 2, may be more frequent for SIL 3
proof_test_procedures.documentation: Proof test report: date, personnel, test method, results, any discrepancies, corrective actions, next test due date

partial_stroke_test.purpose: Test SIS valve movement without fully shutting down the process — proves the valve can move and is not stuck
partial_stroke_test.method: Command valve to move 10-20% from normal operating position, verify it moves, then return to normal position
partial_stroke_test.frequency: Monthly or quarterly between full proof tests — extends the proof test interval by taking credit for PST diagnostic coverage
partial_stroke_test.what_it_proves: Valve is not stuck, actuator works, positioner responds, solenoid valve operates
partial_stroke_test.what_it_does_NOT_prove: Full valve shutoff, correct fail position under total air/power loss, correct trip point at sensor
partial_stroke_test.smart_positioner_PST: Many modern positioners have built-in PST function — can be triggered from DCS without field visit
partial_stroke_test.documentation: Record date, valve position commanded, actual position achieved, pass/fail, any friction or stiction observed

sil_verification.SIL_1: PFDavg 0.01 to 0.1 (90-99% availability) — single instrument loop, standard hardware
sil_verification.SIL_2: PFDavg 0.001 to 0.01 (99-99.9%) — redundant sensors (1oo2), SIL-rated equipment, proof test interval critical
sil_verification.SIL_3: PFDavg 0.0001 to 0.001 (99.9-99.99%) — high redundancy (2oo3), diverse technology, very tight proof test intervals, hardware fault tolerance 1 minimum
documentation: SIL verification report showing PFD calculation meets target SIL
Safety requirements specification (SRS) for each SIF
Proof test procedure approved by SIS engineer
Device SIL certificates from manufacturers (FMEDA reports)

trip_point_verification: Verify each sensor trip point matches the cause and effect diagram
Apply known input at trip point — verify logic solver detects trip condition
Verify correct output action occurs (right valve closes, right pump stops)
Verify voting logic works correctly (1oo2, 2oo3) — test each sensor individually AND in combination
Reset logic tested: system must require manual reset after trip (auto-reset on safety systems is typically prohibited)

response_time_testing.definition: Total time from process variable reaching trip point to final element achieving safe state
response_time_testing.budget: Sensor response + transmission delay + logic solver scan time + solenoid response + valve stroke time

typical_components.transmitter_response: 100-400 ms (damping setting affects this — reduce damping for SIS transmitters)
typical_components.logic_solver_scan: 50-100 ms per scan (some systems guarantee fixed scan time for SIS)
typical_components.valve_stroke_time: 2-10 seconds depending on size and actuator (THIS is usually the largest component)

response_time_testing.total_must_be_less_than: The process safety time (time from detection to hazardous condition) specified in the SRS
response_time_testing.common_problem: Transmitter damping set too high (slow response) — SIS transmitters should have damping at minimum or per SIS engineer specification
